perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall with regard to any Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement by or on behalf of a Disclosing Party on or after the Effective Date: a. hold the Information in confidence and, except as is otherwise stated herein or agreed in writing by the Disclosing Party, shall not disclose or make available the Information by publication or otherwise to any third p arty (including for the avoidance of doubt, disclosure in any patent application or to any patent office) and shall use any Information disclosed to it pursuant to this Agreement only for carrying out the Purpose;
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall with regard to any Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement by or on behalf of a Disclosing Party on or after the Effective Date: a. endeavor to hold the Information in confidence and, except as is otherwise stated herein or agreed in writing by the Disclosing Party, shall not actively disclose the Information by publication or otherwise to any third party (including for the avoidance of doubt, disclosure in any patent application or to any patent office) and shall use any Information disclosed to it pursuant to this Agreement only for broadly defined purposes;
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'hold the Information in confidence' with 'endeavor to hold the Information in confidence' weakens the obligation. The term 'actively disclose' provides an avenue for passive disclosure claims and the phrase 'broadly defined purposes' replaces a well-defined term.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 4 of the UK Trade Secrets Regulations requires companies to take "reasonable steps" to protect trade secrets. Changing the original text to 'endeavor to hold the information' weakens that requirement and replaces 'Purpose' with the very ambiguous 'broadly defined purposes', which would not qualify as a 'reasonable step' to maintain the confidentiality of trade secrets.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 18 / PART I / CHAPTER 1 / § 4 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 18 USC 4 : Misprision of felony Text contains those laws in effect on July 12, 2025 From Title 18-CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I-CRIMES CHAPTER 1-GENERAL PROVISIONS Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments §4. Misprision of felony Whoever, having knowledge of the actual commission of a felony cognizable by a court of the United States, conceals and does not as soon as possible make known the same to some judge or other person in civil or military authority under the United States, shall be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than three years, or both. ( June 25, 1948, ch. 645, 62 Stat. 684 ; Pub. L. 103–322, title XXXIII, §330016(1)(G), Sept. 13, 1994, 108 Stat. 2147 .) H
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Receiving Party to 'hold the Information in confidence', which establishes a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation by using 'endeavor to hold the Information in confidence' and 'broadly defined purposes'. The Law Explanation states that Section 4 of the UK Trade Secrets Regulations requires companies to take 'reasonable steps' to protect trade secrets. 'Endeavor to hold' and 'broadly defined purposes' would not qualify as a 'reasonable step' to maintain confidentiality. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 are irrelevant. This weakening of the obligation creates a significant contradiction because it replaces a clear duty with a vague aspiration, undermining the legal protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: take all necessary and proper security precautions (and at least as great as those it takes to safeguard its own information) to safeguard every part of the Information to prevent it from being disclosed or otherwise made available to any third party except as permitted by this Agreement; and
perturbation.changed_text: take security precautions it deems appropriate (but not necessarily as great as those it takes to safeguard its own information) to safeguard parts of the Information it considers sensitive to prevent it from being disclosed or otherwise made available to certain third parties except as permitted by this Agreement; and
perturbation.explanation: The modified text makes the security precautions subjective ('deems appropriate') and no longer requires at least the same level of security as the Receiving Party uses for its own information. Furthermore, it only applies to 'parts of the Information it considers sensitive' and 'certain third parties', introducing significant ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 4 of the UK Trade Secrets Regulations mandates 'reasonable steps' to protect trade secrets. Subjective security precautions, a lower standard than the Receiving Party's own protections, and only applying to 'parts' of the information that are sensitive do not constitute 'reasonable steps' to prevent unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: U.S.C. Title 18 - CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE 18 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 18 - CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I - CRIMES CHAPTER 1 - GENERAL PROVISIONS Sec. 4 - Misprision of felony From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §4. Misprision of felony Whoever, having knowledge of the actual commission of a felony cognizable by a court of the United States, conceals and does not as soon as possible make known the same to some judge or other person in civil or military authority under the United States, shall be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than three years, or both. (June 25, 1948, ch. 645, 62 Stat. 684; Pub. L. 103–322, title XXXIII, §330016(1)(G), Sept. 13, 1994, 108 Stat. 2147.) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 18, U.S.C. 1940 ed., §251 (Mar. 4, 1909, ch. 321, §146, 35 Stat. 1114). Changes in phraseology only. Amendments 1994 —Pub. L. 103–322 substituted “fined under this title” for “fined not more than $500”.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities regarding the required security measures, potentially leading to a violation of the UK Trade Secrets Regulations, which mandate 'reasonable steps' to protect trade secrets. By making security precautions subjective ('deems appropriate') and limiting them to 'parts of the Information it considers sensitive' and 'certain third parties,' the modified text undermines the standard of 'reasonable steps' as it provides loopholes that could be exploited.
